. This paper introduces Figaro-GPU that builds on the Figaro algo-

v  for the SVD decomposition.
i
Applications. QR and SVD decompositions lie at the core of
X many linear algebra techniques, and their machine learning appli-
r
cations [3, 12, 13] such as the matrix (pseudo) inverse and the least-
a
squares needed in the closed-formsolution of linear regression. An
SVD decomposition can be used for the principal component anal-
ysis of a matrix.
The matrix R in a QR decomposition constitutes a Cholesky de-
composition of J⊤J, which is used for training (non)linear regres-
sion models using gradient descent [10]. If the matrix J is square,

1, and continue applying Givens rotations by zeroing out remain-

𝑚  𝑚  𝑛  𝑛  2  1

O((  +  ) · (  +  ) )
approach is a simpliﬁcation of a general Figaro algorithm.
We paralleize the simpliﬁed Figaro algorithm as Figaro-GPU
on a GPU in the following way. First, we store the input matri-
ces A and B row-major and compute the respective columns in
the resulting heads and tails using one to one mapping to Cuda
threads. Then we concatenate the heads and tails using Cuda ker-
nels, change the row-major to column-major using cublasXgeam
and ﬁnish the upper triangular matrix using cusolverDnXgeqrf.
We compute singular values by using cusolverDnXgesvd method
on the upper triangular matrix.

2
We evaluate the runtime performance of Figaro-GPU against NVIDIA
cuSolver that use versions 12.6 and 12.8 using the cuSolver C++
API. NVIDIA cuSolver implements the Householder algorithm for
the QR decomposition of dense matrices [5].
We ran experiments on NVIDA 2070 8GiB Cuda 12.8 WSL2 and
NVIDIA 4080 16GiB Cuda 12.6 Ubuntu 20.04.
The performance numbers are averages over 4 consecutive runs.
We do not consider the time to load the database into VRAM and
assume that all relations and the join output are sorted by their join
R𝑚 𝑛
attributes. We use the synthetic datasets of relations S, T  ×  ,
∈
whose join is the Cartesian product. The data in each column fol-
lows a uniform distribution in the range  0, 1 .
(  )
Computing R. We can see in Figure 1, as we scale the num-
ber of columns, the speed-up increases since we paralelize compu-
tations column-wise and the memory access pattern is L2-cache
friendlier for the Figaro-GPU. Furthermore, as we scale the num-
ber of rows, the speed-up only increases since the ratio of the join
output and join input only increases.
Computing Singular Values. We can see in Figure 2 that if
we compute the singular values that the similar reasoning as for
computing the upper triangular matrix holds.

QR decomposition. There are three classical approaches to QR
decomposition of a matrix A: using Givens rotations [2] — zeros
entry by entry of the matrix A only aﬀecting the two rows, while
keeping the existing zero entries in the matrix Aunmodiﬁed; using
Householder transformations [5] —zeros multiple entries in a col-
umn at once while aﬀecting all other entries in the aﬀected rows,
Gram-Schmidt process [4, 11] computes the orthogonal columns of
Q one at a time by subtracting iteratively from the 𝑖-th column of A
all of its projections onto the previously computed 𝑖  1 orthogonal
−
columns of Q.
SVDdecomposition. The approach to computing the SVD that
is closest to ours proceeds as follows [3, p. 285], [6]. It computes
the matrices R and Q in the QR decomposition of A, followed by
the computation of the SVD of R = U𝑅Σ𝑅 𝑉𝑅. Finally, it computes
⊤
the orthogonal matrix U = QU𝑅. The SVD of A is then UΣ𝑅 𝑉𝑅.
⊤
When only singular values are required, the squareroot-free or
dqds method is the most popular [1, 9].

In this paper, we presented Figaro-GPU, a novel algorithm for com-
puting QR and SVD decomposition on GPUs. We experimentally
veriﬁed that Figaro-GPU outperforms in the runtime performance
NVIDIA cuSolver for computing the upper triangular matrix for:
2070 and 4080 and for the singular values for 4080.
